tionally levelling themselves;-think it not, friends
and brethren; you have been and are still intended

you; they think you veriest carrion, but, nevertheless,
have disdained not to fatten on you; verily, they

defend their exclusive spoil, they make way for the
hungry crows, whose numbers trouble them, to come

of spiders that run about the sides of brick walls
in hot summer weather, for the purpose of searching for prey.
They are of a grey colour, have flat bodies, and short legs. There
is nothing particularly striking in their appearance, except that they
have long scraggy necks, and extraordinary large heads, very much
resembling that of a counsellor, when he gets on his lop-ear'd wig.
They have six eyes; two in front, two on the crown, and two on
the back part of their heads, so that they can see in all directions
at one and the same time. They have no regular web, but straggle
about from place to place. They spin a single thread, which they

Our course of duty lays straight before us; let us, one and all,
unite in aiding the glorious cause of liberty, and truth and justice
must prevail.
Reformers and honest men rally round the standard of justice,
and support your unflinching advocate, and the day, we trust, is not
far distant, when "all empty ranks will be abolished-and King,
Slave, and Lord, enobled into Man."
I am, your constant friend and admirer,
August 31, 1831.
H. B.

Sir,
We the working, consequently the useful classes of Blackburn,
in the county of Lancaster, in public meeting assembled, beg leave
to offer you our grateful thanks for the noble, courageous, and
patriotic stand which you have made for the REAL Freedom of the

stifle the free expression of public opinion, and to rob the poor man
of the best means of obtaining useful and correct knowledge of poli-
tical science, will, by your unconquerable and unflinching persever-
ance and resistance, aided and assisted by the people, be completely

INTRODUCTION.
The North American Republic consists of twenty-four regularly
organized States, which with the exception of some mountain ridges
is wholly susceptible of cultivation. It contains upwards of two
millions of square miles, is therefore thirteen times as large as
France; twenty-three times as large as England, and would, were
it peopled as densely as those countries, contain four hundred
millions of people. It is watered on its eastern, western, and on
part of its southern sides by the ocean, and on part of its northern
irontier by immense lakes. It is intersected in all directions by
magnificent rivers, and has more facilities for water conveyance
than any other continental country, it abounds in mines and minerals,
and produces every thing necessary for the sustenance and enjoy-
ment of mankind.
The population of the United States has been several times
doubled, in periods of less than twenty-five years, it now contains
upwards of twelve millions of people, and bids fair at no very
distant period, to be more populous and powerful than any nation
ever was.
Such is the present state, such the present prospects of a nation
originally settled by men who either fled from religious intolerance
and persecution to seek peace in the wilderness, or men whose
notions of liberty were too high to permit them to endure the tyranny
they were subjected to at home; or of men who sought their for-

nt. But all the lead-
ng newspapers of the day are more notorious for their vacil-
lation than for any other character, consequently truth cannot
have been the constant guide of those who write them. The
columns of the Times, the Alor ning Chronicle, the Morning
Herald, and most other daily papers, if reviewed but for a few
months back, will be found full of astonishing incongruities.
Are these editors ignorant men who have wandered from the
path of truth inadvertently, or are they venal prostitutes who
have contaminated their fingers with base bribes? Perhaps
both.
The Sunday papers, with only about two exceptions, collect
the scattered rays of the diurnals into a focus, and reflect them
hebdomadally to illuminate our minds. Rays of light passing
through an impure medium become less irradiating, and the
sentiments issuing from the daily press are seldom presented
to Sunday readers unimpaired by transmission.
The Weekly Dispatch is the most popular weekly paper.

rights divine
Of Lings, or lords, or holy swine ;
But rights of all who dare be free,
Rights founded on Equality!
On Sunday hungry minds are fed,
For Science then her dainties spread,
Like solar rays, or morning dews,
To crowded galleries and pews;
Not at the feast for Persia won
By Philip's sanguinary son ;
Net Bacchus, (Jove's fire rescued brat)
When he knocked off Medusa's hat;
Not Cleopatra of renewa,
Who prov'd Mark Anthony a clown;
Not George, that Epicurean clod,
Nor Cannibals who eat their God;
E'er tasted such delicious fare,
As Mind receives from Knowledge there.
LETTER TO MR. H. HETHERINGTON.
DEAR SIR,-You will be astonished, and I have no doubt it will
afford you much gratification, to see by the accompanying numbers
of the Radical Reformer's Gazette, that the tree of liberty has begun
to put forth its fruits on the Scottish soil. Another volunteer has
been added to the few who have embarked in the cause of civil and
religious freedom, and another battery has been erected to assail the
enemies of the oppressed and plundered many, and to assist in giving
the last fatal blow to the tottering fabric of corruption.

CAIN.
Why do I exist?
Why art thou wretched? Why are all things so?
Ev'n he who made us must be, as the Maker
Of things unhappy! To produce destruction
Can surely never be the task of joy,
And yet my sire says he's omnipotent:
Then why is evil-he being good? I ask'd
This question of my father; and he said,
Because this evil was the path
To good. Strange good, that must arise from out
Its deadly opposite. I lately saw
A lamb stung by a reptile: the poor suckling
Lay foaming on the earth, beneath the vain
And piteous bleating of its restless dam:
My father pluck'd some herbs, and laid them to
The wound; and by degrees the helpless wretch
Resumed its careless life, and rose to drain
The mother's milk, who o'er it tremulous
Stood licking its reviving limbs with joy.
Behold, my son! said Adam, how from evil
Springs good!

Oh these prejudices! which ever tend to error! May those
works live which breathe the inspiration of truth. They are the
light-they are the truth. Elsewhere, under despotism and arbitrary
power, all is dark. But I am certain that the knowledge of my pro-
ductions will dispel the clouds which obscure the light. For the tros
light must spring from the love of liberty and equality—it can ema-
nate from no other source.
Nature, as far as concerns the human race, has placed those
principles in the heart; but man must develope and perfect them.
Even now, men are philosophers more from custom than reason.
They accommodate themselves to the customs of the people amonget
whom they live.
This is Philosophy-the means of its existence proceeds from the
fruit of its own labour. It admits not of the terms-master or ser-
vant; for as it is impossible to make a peacock's tail out of that of
a pig, it is equally impossible to make a philosopher out of a titled
man!
The Philosopher calls all men brothers and friends. He regards
the law of nature as the law of the strongest. That the conquerer is
the master of the conquered-that he can treat his slave and his
prisoner according to his will. But if nature protects the vanquished
-till be becomes the conqueror-he has the same right. This is
what all men ought to learn by heart, and put into action. To reflect

ORIGINAL POETRY.
They that be slain with the sword are better than they that be
elain with hunger; for these pine away, stricken through for want of
the fruits of the field.-Lamentations, ch. iv. v. 9.
Ah! wherefore weep my mother land,
Since Pharbus smiles with kindly ray,
And summer strews with bounteous hand
The joyous earth with flow rets gay?
Some in pleasure's car at eve'a,
Thro' the park and o'er the lake,
By zephyr breath or œlus driven,
In satiate draughts of sorrow make.
Yet others gnaw the clankless chain
Of want, which binds a free-born race,
Who, fetter'd, moan to bite again
Those links Britannia's muse disgrace.
The sickle comes, and nature yields
Her harvest's fruits and vineyard's wine,
And till a sword each subject wields,
To servile death devoted pine.

shining lights in a world of darkness, and temples as sanctuaries of

in which so many nations have found a moral death. Man, I say, is

Lawyers and Spiders.
One day, upon removing some books at the chambers of Sir Wil-
liam Jones, a large spider dropped upon the for, upon which Sir
William, with some warmth, said, "Kill that spider, Day; kill that
spider!" No, (said Mr. Day, with coolness) I will not kill that
spider, Jones: I do not know that I have a right to kill that spider.
Suppose, when you are going in your coach to Westminster Hall, a
superior Being, who perhaps may have as much power over you as
you have ever that insect. should say to his companion, Kill that
lawyer! kill that lawyer! how should you like that, Jones? and I
am sure, to most people, a lawyer is a more noxious animal than a
spider."
THE FACTORY CHILD.
I bear the blithe voices of children at play,
And the sweet birds rejoicing on every green spray;
On all things the bright beams of summer have smiled,
But they smile not on me--the poor Factory Child.
The gay sports cf childhood to me they deny,
And the fair paths of learning I never must try;
A companion of creatures whom guilt has defiled,
Oh, who does not pity the Factory Child.

It is pretty generally admitted, that when God bade man go
forth and multiply and replenish the earth, he meant him to
enjoy the same free and unrestrained. If this was the Divine
intention-and the Bible says something remarkably like it-
then we are quite certain that in many countries the benevolent
desire has been wofully frustrated. Man hath found out many
things, but the worst of all is, that which has enabled one class
to seize upon the great common of nature, and to hold it to the
exclusion of all their brethren. On this island, which it was
meant should maintain the whole animated dust upon it, one
million of creatures live in luxuriousness, while fifteen millions
scramble for crumbs. There is no concealing the fact, that in
England the rich are far too rich-the poor far too poor; and
that the law which tends to create and maintain this inequality
must be amended. Knowledge is removing the scale from
men's eyes, and the time is coming in which the vision of the
poet will be made manifest, and the motto of nations be-
A man's a man for a' that.

muuity. The time for fair and soft speech, as well as for half mea-

it seems a mere mass of rottenness, it still possesses stamina enough

to exbibit the disease, without at the same time pointing out the
remedy.
THE ABOMINATION OF ENGLAND.
Another marked feature in the Agitator will be to shew up the
corruptions and oppressions of the Church, and the profligacy and
crimes of the Clergy, together with the melancholy effects of that
lax system of morality which disgraces all sorts and conditions of
men who have been taught by a sycophant priesthood that it is com-
patible to worship, at one and the same time, their God and Mam-
Dion. The Agitator will always bave an eye to the everlasting law
of righteousness, as distinct from the ever-shifting rule of bypocrites,
who make their show of religion serve only as a stepping stone to
their worldly welfare; and bence, no less for their sake than our
own, we shall advocate the abolition of tithes and other equally strong
motives to imposture on the part of the clergy, who, if they are to be
supported, ought to look to their heavenly father to feed them, as be
did Elisha with rook pies, or the Israelites with the still more ex-
quisite manna of the wilderness.

industry, to the great satisfaction of connoisseurs, when another
Signor, named Maestro, contrived to creep into the cabinet of the

capacity may understand them as clearly as it understands that two
and two make four.

consulted. A majority of the numerous rules given in most Gram-

Government of usurers,-and that means, a Goveru-
ment of Hell. It is essentially criminal,-for its
scope and design is, to enable one section of the
community to prey like cannibals upon the others.
It is also essentially ignorant,-for it proceeds upon
the supposition, that the middle classes are happier
under the present system, than they would be under
a social one, based on justice and humanity. This
is a fatal error. The middle classes cau never bo
happy under the cannibal system. Even in a pecu-
niary sense, the majority are worse off than they
would be in a social community. It is an admitted
fact, that in the present advanced state of science,
every adult human being can, with moderate labour,
and under judicious arrangements, produce five times
more of the goods of life than he needs to consume.
Can the middle classes command this abundance
now? Certainly not. A few possess it, and many
times more: but the majority are as much pinched,
considering their wants and habits, as the working
classes themselves.

PROPOSITIOS FOR THE CONSOLIDATION OF
THE PRODUCTIVE CLASSES.
[There is a great deal of matter and good sense in the fol-
lowing propositions, which we take from the Official Gazette of
the Consolidated Union. We recommend them to the attentive
perusal of our readers.]-ED.
PROPOSITIONS.
1.-There is no species of wealth, or, in other words, no
article of real and substantial value that is not, more or less, the
product of labour.
2.—Money is not real wealth, but only its representative, and
a medium of exchange or barter. Its only value consists in
the
power it confers upon its possessor, of obtaining in exchange
for it articles of real value. Hence there may be money where
there is no really valuable article. Money will not itself feed
the hungry, or clothe the naked.
3.-The possession of money is not absolutely necessary,
therefore, to the possession and enjoyment of real wealth, which
consists, as we have said, in articles of intrinsic utility.
4.-By far the greater part of the population of the United
Kingdom consists of persons who have no means of subsistence,
but those created by their own labour.
5.-The labouring classes may be divided into two, namely,
the producers and the distributors; the former are the agents of
creation, the latter of exchange or d

THE LANGUAGE OF ANIMALS."That animals have each a
language of their own to one another," says James Hogg, in his
Sermons," there can be no doubt. I know by the voice of the
raven when he has discovered one of my flock dead-I know also
his prelude to the storm and to fine weather. The moor-fowls
call one another from hill to hill. I learned to imitate their lan-
guage so closely that I could have brought scores of them within
the range of my shot of a morning. The black cock has a call,
too, which brings all his motley mates around him, but the fe-
males have no call. They are a set of subordinate beings, like
the wives of a nabob. They dare not even incubate upon the
same hill with their haughty lords. But the partridge, and every
mountain bird, have a language to each other, and though rather
circunscribed, it is perfectly understood, and, as Wordsworth
says, not to me unknown. Even the stupid and silly barn-
door hen,

that intellect has wings,
And sometimes limp'd by fervor, springs
A little way beyond the bound,
Where sober Reason may be found;
And sometimes, led by the sublime,
Will revel in a brighter clime;
And breath a more celestial fire
Than grosser natures could respire:
And walk through many a airy hall,
Whose floors are thinner than the cloud,
Through which the mists of morning fall,
Whose texture by a tear were bow'd.
You have your followers--some there be,
Who build upon your theory,
Most beauteous structures I peopled, too,
With beings of a kindred hue;
Worthy to be compar'd, at least,
With the bright Peries of the East;
Putting our Fairies quite to shame,
As creatures of a rustic name;
Whose moon-light revels needs must be
Disgrace to a community,
Of mortals much more light and gay
Than ever Fairy was, or. Fay.
Some such, indeed, there be, who think
They stand, even now, upon the brink
Of change; produced by what effect
They know not; but they still expect
A gorgeous palace will be seen
On Turnham, or on Tottenham Green,
Next summer, where they shall reside,
With all that clings to wealth, save pride
Engaged in what pursuit may please→→→
Labour or sport, or happy ease;
A book, or beauty, as may suit
The moment; wine, or foreign fruit.
In sooth, whate'er the world can give,
That mortal in delight should live.
Teach those men as best you can,
How circumstances act on man,
And time on circumstances. Teach
Them not to strive at once to reach
The topmost branches of the tree,
But

that is, (in plain English,) that it makes them earu
their bread. Now this, in our opinion, is doing a

Fluttering falls the wounded bird;
Off the watchful spaniels run,
Back they come at sportsman's word.

Now are levell'd with the clods!

MIGHT IS RIGHT," 'tis shortly said;
The man has power, the game has not,
He points his gun and shoots it dead.
And argument availeth not,
And reason's wasted upon power;
To fear alone 'twill yield a jot,
Nor that until the eleventh hour,
But who are these in russet jackets,

Shot-belts, dogs, and powder-flaskets,

Of orders, privileged beyond
Those of bare walls and empty cupboard,
To shoot the game and fish the pond,
Whence this proud privilege-its rise?
'Mongst men of the same common clay;.
From money sprung; it has its price,
Which price a poor man cannot pay.
Though pay he cannot, eat he can

IMPROVEMENT IN PADDLE-W¡IEELS.-A small model of a
new invention in this way has been sent to our office for inspec-
tion, accompanied by a letter from the inventor, pointing out
the differences between it and the common paddle-wheel, and
its superiority over them in various points; the most mate-
rial of which is, that the paddles in the machine turn upon two
pivots placed at the end, and work in holes at the sides or arms
of the wheel; and, from being thus fixed, the paddles would
all remain perpendicular when the wheel is at rest; these over-
balance from the pivot being downwards, if not prevented by
stop-pers. When in motion the paddle is kept firmly against
the pers until it passes the bottom or perpendicular line, when
the overbalance, which, from the pivot, is upwards, will gently
turn it over, and it will rise perpendicular, with its thin edge
out of the water, leaving the latter without any back wave or
swell: it is, therefore, formed on the principle of not lifting the
hack wave.
Two patents have lately been taken out for paddle-
wheels to prevent the sea-lift; and trials are about to be made
of them upon the largest scale; and this, certainly, is the only
true method of ascertaining their capabilities. The maker of
this machine signs himself James Carol, 38, Tufton-street, West-
minster, where he will show the machine each day from ten
until two o'clock.-Herald.

ich, near Manchester, on Saturday,
they surprized an immense spider, which crawled out of its hole.
It is supposed to be the great American spider. Its body is
about two inches and a half long, and about an inch thick;
colour a dark-brown. It has ten legs of great length, and eight
eyes; its nippers are black, horny, and sharp, but not serrated;
they were scarcely discernible, until a piece of quill was in-
serted, and then we found them to contain a small house-fly,
which the insect must have caught after its exhumation. There
are no antennæ visible. Its body is partially covered with a
soft down, and partly with a very long and strong red hair: it
weighs within a few gains of half an ounce. But the great sin-
gularity is the habitation which this insect has occupied probably
for years. Mr. Eames purchased the logwood in which it was
found two or three months ago at Liverpool, and be thinks it not
unlikely that the wood may have been two or three years inj

In America there is no Louis Philippe, yet even
there the middle classes do without him what their
bretheren in France do with him. In America, as in
-England, the question of labour and capital is attract-
ing attention, and in America, as in England, there
are" Helots" and "Patriots" who try every stratagem
to beguile the working classes from it, and to fix their
attention on barren and superficial projects. The fol-
lowing, from a New York Paper, will show we are
not falsifying or exaggerating. It is from an article
headed humbugs," and the paper is the "Working
Man's Advocate.'
"There are every day to be found among us some fresh humbugs,
large and small gull traps, to catch the unwary; but such new-fan-
gled conceits are not dangerous: a few butterflies lose their wings,
and some half a dozen flies their legs, and the trick is exposed. There
are, however, bumbugs of longer standing which have the sanction
of antiquity, that are truly dangerous; and the most pernicious of
these are sanctioned by the laws of the land. Humbugs sustained by
law are dangerous, because the people are not allowed to examine
and pronounce an opinion upon them. There is not a sensible man
in the community who will acknowledge that he is ruled by laws that
he never understood, and never hopes to understand, or that these
laws are introduced, interpreted, and put into execution by a combi-

in the Charing-cross Hospital, detailing the particulars) of the
melancholy and affecting case of the poor fellow who was bitten

who died and was dissected in the hospital last month. As the
case is one of the highest moment relative to a disease which has
as yet baffled all the efforts of the most experienced practitioners,
we this day give a brief outline of Dr. Golding's lecture.
Before detailing the symptoms, progress, and result of the
case alludad to, and the appearances which presented themselves
upon a post-mortem examination, he entered into a review of the
opinions hitherto entertained of this frightful affection, and the
various plans of treatment had recourse to by the most celebrated
of the medical profession, English and continental, for its relief.
The result of this deduction was, that every remedy that had
been attempted had proved entirely unavailing, and that the
nature of the malady was atill involved in mystery.
Dr. Golding from the result of his own observations, regarded
it as a peculiar affection of the nervous system, arising from
specific stiumlous contained in the malignant virus, and thought
the only preventive would be the excision of the parts: and that,
however desirable this might be, directly the injury had been
received from the bite of the animal, yet, as the virus seemed to
remain inert for a longer or a shorter period, he considered that

PITY.-Sir John Suckling used to say, "I pity the poet who
bas to write for his bread. I pity the man who has fallen into
the hands of a pettyfogging attorney. I pity the man who is mar-
ried to a scold, unless he is deaf. I pity the woman who is
married to a rake, unless she is blind. I pity the man who is
in debt, and would pay if he could. I pity the man who can
only boast of a long pedigree."
ANIMALS TURNED AUTHORS-If animals were to turn an-
thors, the eagle would excel in epic, and the sheep in pastoral
postry. The elephant would produce an excellent treatise on
philosophy; the horse employ his genius on cavalry-the cow
ou agriculture; and the dog cut a figure in the drama. The
writings of the monkey would abound in satire and burlesque ;
while the cat would be distinguished for the sarcasm, envy,
and disingenuousness of his compositions. The style of the
lion would be bold, abrupt, and Pindaric; while the gander
would be reinarkable for the extreme verbosity and diffuseness
of his language. The badger would probably attempt a trea-
tise on the medical effect of perfumes; the turkey a disqui-
sition on the mock-heroic. The genius of the owl would ex-
hibit itself in the composition of elegies, epitaphs, and solemn
dirges; that of the bear in an Essay on Waltzing. As for the
hog, he could never excel in polite literature, but might favour
the world with a critical analysis on the Philosophy of Baco

PICTURE OF A POLAR WINTER. About the middle of
November we began to feel the approach of a Polar winter. A
feeble gleam of light still faintly tinged the southern horizon,
while the opposite direction assumed the sombre shade of night.
The almost dazzling whiteness of the grotesque hummocks of ice,
that lay scattered in irregular masses on the plane, formed a
strange contrast with the soft, purple shade of twilight. The
full moon shed its pale lastre on the lonely scene, and the colours
which it reflected on the ice were chaste beyond conception. The
planets twinkled in their orbs, and Jupiter shene brilliantly at
the hour our friends in the English Channel might be taking a
meridian altitude of the sun. The aurora borealis was often

ever beheld, rising in a single line of brilliant light in the south

EXTRAORDINARY SEA-FISH POND IN SCOTLAND.-At Port
Nes-ock, in Wigtonshire, a large salt-water pond has been
formed for cod. It is a basin of 30 feet in depth and 160 feet
in circumference, hewn out from the solid rock, and com-
municating with the sea by one of those fissures which are
common to bold and precipitous coasts. Attached to it is a neat
gothic cottage for the accommodation of the fishermen, and the
rock is surmounted all round by a substantial stone wall 300
feet in circumference. From the inner or hack door of the lodge
a winding stairway conducts to the usual halting-place-a large
flat stone, projecting into the water, and commanding a view of
every part of the aquatic prison. When the tide is out the stone
is left completely dry, and here a stranger perceives with sur-
prise a hundred mouths simultaneously opened to greet his
arrival. The moment the fisherman crosses his threshold the
pond is agitated by the action of some hundred fins, and other-
wise thrown into a state of anarchy and confusion. Darting
from this, that, and the other corner, the whole population move
as it were to a common centre, elerate their snouts, lash their
tails, and jostle one another with such violence that on a first
view they actually seem to be menacing an attack on the poor
fisherman in place of the creel-full of

For the Guardian.
Tune, "There's nae luck about the house."
pictor, aliptes
Medicus, Magas omnis novit.
IN Erin's land of sport and fight,
Of frolic, fun, and mirth,
There Quacking Long first saw the light
La lighting on this earth;
The time, too, history us acquaints
Midsummer day, at one;
So born within the Isle of Saints,
They christen'd him St. John.
An humble life his father led,
In science deep unlearned,
He baskets made for others' bread,
And thus his own he earned.
No undiscover'd paths he found,
No substitute for sallow,
Por ever dipp'd in arts profound,
Although he work'd in Tullow.
Some symptoms soon of future fame,
Like Hercles, Long displayed,
For nought unto his cradle came
But quickly it was flayed;
And as great Ammon's son, when young,
Two snakes deprived of breath,
So by two fleas to madness stung,
John rubb'd them both to death.
Nay more, the urchin, some folks say,
His mother's face would claw,
And where his nails he once did lay,
He 'stablished there a raw;
And 'twas in vain his parents strove,
Dame Nature's bent concealing,
For while bis aire the osiers wove,
Young Long their skins was peeling.
Then how to check his instinct's force,
The pair were sorely hobbled,
So John they bound-a last resource-
Unto a man that cobbled.
But nought with Nature's strength can match,
And soon his friends did feel it;
For though his work be'd quick despatch,
'Twas found he ne'er could heelt it.
And now to tempt sublimer arts,
And fy his dad's rebukes,
John chang

issued that,
after the present year, no more prizes are to be given for chrono-
meters deposited for trial at the Royal Observatory. It has
been a practice, for the last fourteen or fifteen years, to receive,
at the Observatory, a number of chronometers, the rates of which
were regularly registered by one of the assistants; and at the
end of a twelve month that chronometer whose rate had been
most equable, entitled its owner to a prize of 300l.; the second
best to 2001.; and the third to 150l.; Government retaining the
instruments for these respective sums. The effect of this plan
has been to produce an excellence in chronometers which pro-
bably no other means would have ensured. Its immediate con-
sequence has been to secure for his Majesty's Navy the finest
time-piece in the world; and as the lives of a thousand men,
and property of the value of 100,000/. in many instances de
pend upon the excellence of one of these instruments of naviga-
tion, the object that has been obtained by the institution of
prizes is one of the highest national importance.

He afterwards extended his labours, and discovered a multitude
of beings, of which the only traces which remain are some scattered
bones. And it is necessary to comprebend all the difficulty of the
task. He had to recognise and to classify all species totally unknown,
and this with fragments of skeletons, with broken and incomplete
bones, mixed up and confounded with those of other animals. Com.
parative anatomy saliced to guide Cuvier in this labyrinth, which
appeared inextricable, and faruished rules for the reconstruction of
all those animals for ever obliterated from the book of life. This, in
itself, was curious, thus to restore to the light of day so many beings
that had been destroyed; but a more singular result struck Cuvier
viz, that not only were they not contemporaneous with our present
species, bat that they were not contemporaneous with each other.
They are deposited in strate, and those which lie the deepest are by
their form the most removed from ours. There have, therefore, been
different epochs of the globe; several renewals of existence; in a
word, distinct creations.
We make use of this term, for science can no longer admit of races
of infinite duration. Cavier distinguisbed three well-marked epocks.

REASON.
Reason, all bail! thou fairest boon of Heaven;
Thou sacred pledge to human nature given;
Prophetic star, that points a life to be ;
Existence in a desert, but for thee:
Science is thine-the vintage of the mind,
The light of life, that digniñes mankind ;
Science, the offspring of the soul's free scope.
Which lends to Truth the genuine fount of Hope.
Oh! may its rays dispel that chilling blast,
The powers of mind by ignorance o'ercast :
May thy true chart, thou guide to erring man,
Expand his soul the path of truth to scan;
Trace from its source the pure unsullied stream,
Free from the glimmerings of mysterious dreams;
Pellucid, pure, yea, as its source, divine,
Untinctured by the rancid ware of time;
Primeval streams upon its marble bel.
With no putrescence and contagion fed :
Bright and reflecting, tho' deep veiled its source,
Its current, Truth-Eternity its course.
Reason! 'tis thou uushrouds the mental skies,
And bids fair Freedom's sacred sun arise:
Without the light how listless would we be,
The mere brute grovelling in mortality!
When thou propels, 'tis Nature's mighty God,
Whose ray illumes and consecrates the road

attained. This opinion, coupled with the fact that politics are

of their want of support
That physical science is of high importance no one can honestly
deny; but who can maintain that it is the most worthy of attention?
Nothing has been more popular as a subject of discourse than the
"march of intellect," and the formation of the Useful Knowledge
Society has been pronounced an era in the annals of knowledge.

STATISTICS OF POISONING IN FRANCE.-It results from
the rescarches of Dr. Chevalier, a Member of the Academy
of Medicine, in Paris, and of M. Boys-de: Loury:-1st. That
within seven years 273 individuals have been tried for adminis-
tering poison; of whom 171 have been acquitted, and 102 con-
demned. 2dly. That the poisons employed were in 54 cases
arsenic, in 7 verdegris, in 5 cautharides, in 5 perchlorure of
mercury, in 4 nux vomica, in 3 powder for the destruction of
flies, in 2 nitric acid, in 1 sulphur of arsenic, in 1 emetic tartar,
in 1 opium, in 1 acetate of lead, in 1 white lead, in 1 sulphuric
acid, in 1 sulphate of zinc, in 1 mercurial ointment, in 5 poi-
sons not named.-3dly. That the assigned motives for the
crimes have been-in 28 cases interest, in 24 libertinism, in 15
vengeance, in 10 jealousy, and in six madness.-4thly. In 28
out of 81 cases, the poison was administered in broth, in 8
cases in milk, 7 in flour, 4 in medicine; twice it was intro-
duced immediately into the mouth, twice in coffee, once in
cider, and once in a fowl. It has been remarked that in many
cases the taste communicated by the poisonous substances has
saved the victims, and that in others the colour of the poison
has been a salutary warning. Hence Messrs. Chevalier and
Boys-de-Loury conclude that poisonings would be less fre-
quent if poisonous substances were coloured or rendered sapid.

THE CRIME OF POISONING.-The French chemists make
the following proposition, in order to render less frequent the
crime of poisoning, and to put on their guard those who may be
marked out as the victims of revenge, jealousy, or the like.
From 1824 to 1832 the number of individuals accused of
poisoning was 273, and it appeared that, in many instances the
intended victims had been saved by the bad taste communicated
to the food by the poisonous substance. It is, therefore, recom-
mended that it should be rendered compulsory to colour or
give a flavour of all poisonous substances which would not be
deteriorated by the admixture. For the latter purposes aloes
have been suggested, and of this many English as well as
French chemists have approved. It has also been recommended
to scent all poisons with the same odour-musk for instance.

STANZAS ON A WHITE HAWTHORN IN FULI. BLOOM
IN A FIELD NEAR RICHMOND HILL.
BY THOMAS MACCONNELL.
SLOWs of the summer months which lay,
Unmelted 'midst May's warm green gush!
Bleach'd vegetable muslin gay!
Dash of great Nature's whitewash brush!
Striking, but unobtrusive, flower,
Belle of the field-the hedges pride;
Of blossoms sweet thou art a shower,
Scent-box of nature open'd wide.
No'er under green-house roof thy head,
Stranger to nursery's fostering power;
To pruning knife thy branch ne'er bled,
Nor water'd thou by mimic shower.
The cloud thy water-pot has been,
Thy pruning knife, the cutting wind;
Tby nurse, the storm-thy fire, the sun;
Thy wooer's cattle,-no: mankind.
Born in the hedge,-bred by the ditch,
Neighbour of aloe, and crab, and briar;
Or weakly woodbine creeping, which
Craves friendship's lift, to get up higher.
Flower of the field! thou'rt not in debt,
One farthing for the care of man;
Lilacs! Libernums! lofty set!
Let him say this of you who can.
Consumptive lilly's sickly white,
And gaudy tulip's blazing bue;
Flaming carnations burning bright,
Deep haughty shades of Flear-de-Leuis!
From your grand glare I to:n my bead,
To field-bleach'd bloom of simple white;
An angel's garb-I'll pluck a stred,
To deck my parlour stove grate bright.
I pluck, but oh! my hand it bleeds

A DOCTORS' "STRIKE-The medical practitioners in various
parts of the country are forming themselves into associations to
resist the terms proposed to them for attendance on the poor,
under the regulations proposed by the Central Commissioners.
The professional gentlemen in East Kent and Buckinghamshire
have entered into spirited resolutions on this subject.-Evening
Paper.
NATURAL PHENOMENON.-On the Chinese frontier, near
Kiakha, while the weather was very calm and extremely cold,
on the 11th of March, there was observed, a few minutes after
9. p. m., at an extraordinary elevation in the north-west, a
fiery meteor in a serpentue form of a most dazzling brightness.
In an instant this meteor was converted into a brilliant cloud,
resembling an immense blazing sheaf, which proceeded to pre-
cipitate itself obliquely with extreme rapidity towards the
earth, assuming the form of an enormous sheet of flame, which
filled all the visible space of the horizon with a light as clear as
day, and, separating into three portions, disappeared. The
Loar of distant thunder was then heard, and this occasioned a
tremulous motion to be felt in the houses. Two other sounds
were perceived, bat these were reckoned merely the echoes
of the first reverberating from the neighbouring mountains.
This phenomenon, which lasted but for a few seconds, has not
occasioned, as far as has yet been ascertained, the least damage.

ge one will be compressed to such a degree, that, with the
load in the car, the whole machine will be rendered specifically

With respect to the mode of propulsion adopted by the
inventors of the "Eagle," I may just state that I do not consider
it by any means the best that could be employed.
I remember hearing a lecture on aërostation delivered by Mr.
Tatum, some years since, in which he proposed to effect aërial
navigation by means of two revolving vanes and a rudder. A
very considerable velocity could be imparted to a pair of vanes,
without so great a loss of power as must necessarily take place in
using wings.
Balloons have for a long time past been mere toys, exhibited
for the sake of gain; and I confess I am glad to see aërostation,
as a science, is not entirely forgotten. There is, doubtless, but a
very limited sphere of usefulness open to balloons; but, I believe,
much more can be accomplished than many persons are at present
prepared to admit. I may return to this subject again by-and-by.
In the mean time,

WATER DRINKING.-It is a great mistake to think that beer
is necessary for a hard-working man. At the time I write,
there are a set of inen employed in draining, by task-work, in
Richmond Park, who are patterns of English labourers. Hard
as they work from morning to night, and in all weathers, they
seldom drink any beer. They boil a large kettle of coffee in
their little bivouac in the park, and drink it hot at their meals.
This costs them but liule; but they do as hard a day's work
upon it as any labourers in England, and have continued to
do so for three years past, under all the disadvantages arising
from wet and cold to which a drainer is subject. A proof of
this may be found in Captain Ross's

text:
on found, from the elevation of the lower part of the
balloon, that his apprehensions were in part realised. He
began to descend with great velocity, and fortunately landed on
a favourable spot, his fall being broken by a pine sapling, on
which he alighted. He was hurled from the car, however, with
considerable force, and was severely jarred by the fall, breaking
his valuable barometer and damaging some of his other instru-
ments. Had he descended in the woods, by which he was
nearly surrounded, he thinks his death would have been inevi-
table, and he evidently had a very narrow escape. On examining
the balloon, it was found to have sustained a rent near the
top, of about 20 inches in length, and several parts in which it
had previously broken and patched had all been re-opened. It
is now completely ruined.
Mr. Hobart, in estimating his great distance from the earth at
26,000 feet, argues from the effects upon his respiration and
hearing, which were much more distressing than those described
by Eronatus at a height of 25,000 feet, beyond which no one
was ever known to ascend.

in BEHALF of ALLEN DAVENPORT.-The name of
this individual is well known in the political world, though, by
the scantiness of his means, he is compelled to live in great
obscurity. At this he was never known to repine; but has been
thrown on a bed of sickness, and snatched from the jaws of
death by the scientific abilities of his medical attendant, and
who, to his everlasting credit, devoted the whole of his attention
to that sole object.
We have great pleasure in stating that the individual in ques-
tion has, by the Friends of his own Order, been enabled to pro-
cure the common necessaries of life, and a small portion of its
comforts; but his recovery being extremely slow, and his pecu-
niary prospects very gloomy, we, his friends, have taken the
liberty of thus appealing to all who may have approved of his
Writings and Conduct as an advocate of the Working Classes.
Should any benevolent person feel disposed to assist him, the
smallest favour will be thankfully, received, and gratefully ac-
knowledged, by the following persons:-Mr. Watson, 18, Com-

Fox-CHACE EXTRAORDINARY IN THE LONDON Docks.-
For some time past the warehousekeepers in the London Ducks
have been at a loss to conceive a reason for the great destruc-
tion of their poultry; the feathers and other portions of the
birds have been found in such questionable shape that at once
proved that it was not of the biped species that caused so great
a havoc. A reward of one sovereign was offered by the late Mr.
Scott for the apprehension of the robber; but, of course, without
effect. The mystery has at length been solved, by the appear-
ance of a sleek and sly fox stealing from the eastern quay and
approaching the north. His appearance certainly at first caused
a little terror, thinking it possible it might have been some wiid
animal escaped from a vessel in the docks; but his true character
was soon ascertained, and the cry of“ A fox, a fox !" burst from
many, and the chace was then given. Reynard bounded along
at a spanking pace; but it was useless, too many opposed and
thwarted his intentions, and at last he was ob iged to take re-

Phillips, who has now the animal in his possession. It fortir

vault; had he entered the same he, no doubt, must have died of

heat that is communicated from her body be unequally distri-
buted, but the yolk would become misplaced. A laying hen,
that has made her own nest, ust do the same; for she could
not lay the requisite number for a brood in a shorter space of
time than 29 days; and in a quarter of that period the yolk
would have surk through the white, and come in contact with
the shell, which, being porous, would have admitted the at-
Inospheric air, the vital principle would have become incr',
and the egg would be addled. Eggs intended for use should
never be suffered to remain in a nest more than one day, be-
cause every additional visit of the hen to deposit their eggs
induces a warmth, which is inimical to keeping, though ne-
cessary to hatching them. As early as the third day of incu-
bation the nature of the egg is altered, and is consequentl
rendered unfit for use. The reader will have discovered that
we think the instinct of the mother bird, or rather nature, has
pointed out the most perfect plan to preserve eggs fresh, and
entirely equal to t'.ose that are now laid. It is indeed effectel
simply by turning every day.-Mag. of Domestic Economy.
